summary: Joe Reis offers practical advice for aspiring public speakers in tech and data, emphasizing that speaking confidence comes from intentional practice rather than innate talent. He recommends starting with low-stakes environments like recording yourself, podcasts, and virtual meetups before progressing to live audiences at local meetups and eventually conference CFPs, while cautioning against over-reliance on slides that can become a crutch for inexperienced speakers.
key_points: Public speaking fear is universal - most people would 'rather die than speak in public' - but can be overcome through intentional practice
Start building confidence at home through self-recording, podcasts, and virtual presentations before moving to live audiences
Avoid slides when starting out - they're 'distracting at best and a crutch at worst' - focus on actual speaking skills first
Local meetups and Toastmasters are ideal training grounds before submitting to conference CFPs
Authentic, quirky speakers are better than those molded into caricatures by speaking coaches
money_quotes: By most accounts, people would rather die than speak in public. The fear is real.
Becoming a good speaker is as much about effective communication as is putting on the blinders to negative self-talk.
Unless the slides contain information critical to your presentation and the audience benefits from visual cues, slides are distracting at best and a crutch at worst.
People don't attend talks to stare at PowerPoints. The talk should be between you and your audience.
I think the best speakers are authentic and quirky. If your speaking coach tries to transform you into something you're not, that's a disservice to you and the audience.
key_insight: Public speaking excellence comes not from eliminating fear but from building confidence through intentional practice, starting in low-stakes environments and gradually progressing while staying authentic to your own voice.
